title_en: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj in Al-Kafi
title_ar: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj in Al-Kafi
title_id: Zakat, Puasa, dan Haji dalam Al-Kafi
content_en:
The chapters on zakah, sawm, and hajj in al-Kafi present the Maliki positions with the hadith grounding that characterizes Ibn Abd al-Barr's approach, making the text valuable both as a legal reference and as an introduction to the evidential basis of Maliki law.

On zakah, al-Kafi covers the standard Maliki positions with their evidential basis. The nisabs for gold, silver, livestock, and agricultural produce are each grounded in the relevant prophetic hadiths and the practice transmitted by the Companions. Ibn Abd al-Barr's mastery of hadith allows him to note which hadiths on zakah are sound, which are disputed, and how Malik navigated the evidential landscape in determining the school's positions.

The Maliki school's distinctive extension of kaffarah to all deliberate nullifiers of the Ramadan fast is grounded in al-Kafi by reference to the underlying principle: the prophetic hadith establishing kaffarah for intercourse demonstrates that the sanctity of the Ramadan fast is protected by a severe expiation, and the Maliki school extends this protection by analogy to all deliberate violations. Ibn Abd al-Barr presents this reasoning and notes the contrary positions of scholars who restrict kaffarah to intercourse alone.

For hajj, al-Kafi presents the Maliki positions on the forms of ihram, the miqat boundaries, and the rites with their evidential basis in the hadith of the Prophet's Farewell Pilgrimage. Ibn Abd al-Barr draws heavily on the comprehensive hadiths that describe the Prophet's own hajj — particularly the detailed account in Sahih Muslim — to ground the Maliki positions on the specific rites and their performance.

On zakah al-fitr, al-Kafi presents the Maliki requirement of food rather than cash with the hadith of Ibn Umar, which specifies the food types that the Prophet required. Ibn Abd al-Barr discusses the scholars' debate about whether the food types mentioned in the hadith are illustrative or exhaustive, and presents Malik's position that the obligation is to provide food of the local staple type, not necessarily one of the hadith's specific types.

The chapter on voluntary fasting presents the Maliki positions on the recommended fasts of the year: the six days of Shawwal (on which Malik was reluctant to recommend fasting because he feared people would think them obligatory), the Day of Arafah for non-pilgrims, the Day of Ashura, and the fasts of the months of Rajab and Sha'ban. Ibn Abd al-Barr presents Malik's distinctive caution about some of these fasts and the hadith evidence for each.
content_id:
Bab-bab mengenai zakat, puasa, dan haji dalam al-Kafi menyajikan posisi Maliki beserta landasan hadis yang menjadi ciri khas pendekatan Ibn Abd al-Barr, menjadikan teks ini bernilai baik sebagai rujukan hukum maupun sebagai pengantar dasar dalil hukum Maliki.

Mengenai zakat, al-Kafi mencakup posisi-posisi standar mazhab Maliki beserta dasar dalilnya. Nisab untuk emas, perak, ternak, dan hasil pertanian masing-masing didasarkan pada hadis-hadis kenabian yang relevan dan praktik yang ditransmisikan oleh para Sahabat. Penguasaan Ibn Abd al-Barr atas hadis memungkinkannya mencatat hadis-hadis zakat mana yang sahih, mana yang diperdebatkan, dan bagaimana Imam Malik menavigasi lanskap dalil dalam menentukan posisi mazhabnya.

Perluasan khas mazhab Maliki atas kafarat kepada semua pembatal puasa Ramadan yang dilakukan dengan sengaja didasarkan dalam al-Kafi dengan mengacu pada prinsip yang mendasarinya: hadis kenabian yang menetapkan kafarat untuk hubungan intim menunjukkan bahwa kesucian puasa Ramadan dilindungi oleh penebusan yang berat, dan mazhab Maliki memperluas perlindungan ini melalui qiyas kepada semua pelanggaran yang disengaja. Ibn Abd al-Barr menyajikan penalaran ini dan mencatat posisi berbeda dari ulama yang membatasi kafarat hanya pada hubungan intim saja.

Untuk haji, al-Kafi menyajikan posisi Maliki tentang bentuk-bentuk ihram, batas-batas miqat, dan berbagai manasik beserta dasar dalilnya dalam hadis Haji Wada Nabi. Ibn Abd al-Barr banyak mengandalkan hadis-hadis komprehensif yang menggambarkan haji Nabi sendiri, khususnya riwayat terperinci dalam Sahih Muslim, untuk mendasarkan posisi Maliki tentang manasik tertentu dan pelaksanaannya.

Mengenai zakat fitrah, al-Kafi menyajikan persyaratan Maliki berupa makanan alih-alih uang tunai dengan hadis Ibn Umar yang merinci jenis-jenis makanan yang diwajibkan Nabi. Ibn Abd al-Barr membahas perdebatan ulama tentang apakah jenis-jenis makanan yang disebutkan dalam hadis bersifat ilustratif atau exhaustif, dan menyajikan posisi Imam Malik bahwa kewajibannya adalah menyediakan makanan pokok setempat, tidak harus salah satu dari jenis-jenis yang disebutkan hadis.

Bab tentang puasa sunnah menyajikan posisi Maliki tentang puasa-puasa yang dianjurkan sepanjang tahun: enam hari di bulan Syawal yang enggan dianjurkan oleh Imam Malik karena khawatir orang-orang menganggapnya wajib, Hari Arafah bagi yang tidak berhaji, Hari Asyura, serta puasa di bulan Rajab dan Syaban. Ibn Abd al-Barr menyajikan kehati-hatian khas Imam Malik tentang beberapa puasa ini beserta dalil hadis untuk masing-masingnya.
content_ar:
The chapters on zakah, sawm, and hajj in al-Kafi present the Maliki positions with the hadith grounding that characterizes Ibn Abd al-Barr's approach, making the text valuable both as a legal reference and as an introduction to the evidential basis of Maliki law.

On zakah, al-Kafi covers the standard Maliki positions with their evidential basis. The nisabs for gold, silver, livestock, and agricultural produce are each grounded in the relevant prophetic hadiths and the practice transmitted by the Companions. Ibn Abd al-Barr's mastery of hadith allows him to note which hadiths on zakah are sound, which are disputed, and how Malik navigated the evidential landscape in determining the school's positions.

The Maliki school's distinctive extension of kaffarah to all deliberate nullifiers of the Ramadan fast is grounded in al-Kafi by reference to the underlying principle: the prophetic hadith establishing kaffarah for intercourse demonstrates that the sanctity of the Ramadan fast is protected by a severe expiation, and the Maliki school extends this protection by analogy to all deliberate violations. Ibn Abd al-Barr presents this reasoning and notes the contrary positions of scholars who restrict kaffarah to intercourse alone.

For hajj, al-Kafi presents the Maliki positions on the forms of ihram, the miqat boundaries, and the rites with their evidential basis in the hadith of النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم's Farewell Pilgrimage. Ibn Abd al-Barr draws heavily on the comprehensive hadiths that describe النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم's own hajj — particularly the detailed account in Sahih Muslim — to ground the Maliki positions on the specific rites and their performance.

On zakah al-fitr, al-Kafi presents the Maliki requirement of food rather than cash with the hadith of Ibn Umar, which specifies the food types that النبي صلى الله عليه وسلم required. Ibn Abd al-Barr discusses the scholars' debate about whether the food types mentioned in the hadith are illustrative or exhaustive, and presents Malik's position that the obligation is to provide food of the local staple type, not necessarily one of the hadith's specific types.

The chapter on voluntary fasting presents the Maliki positions on the recommended fasts of the year: the six days of Shawwal (on which Malik was reluctant to recommend fasting because he feared people would think them obligatory), the Day of Arafah for non-pilgrims, the Day of Ashura, and the fasts of the months of Rajab and Sha'ban. Ibn Abd al-Barr presents Malik's distinctive caution about some of these fasts and the hadith evidence for each.
